clean_story:
Future as a mirror. Imagination with rules.

The term science-fiction merges "science" (testable ideas) and "fiction" (invented stories). It’s not just spaceships or lasers—it’s a toolkit for asking, "What if this discovery went further?" like sketching blueprints for tomorrow using today’s physics.

Early sci-fi disguised questions as adventures. Mary Shelley’s Frankenstein (1818) wasn’t about monsters; it was the first story to ask: If we rebuild life, who owns the soul? Later, Jules Verne’s From the Earth to the Moon (1865) predicted spaceflight by calculating trajectories with 19th-century math—his "space cannon" was wrong, but his orbit physics were eerily close to Apollo 11’s.

The genre’s power lies in precision. Hard sci-fi (e.g., Andy Weir’s The Martian) treats science like a puzzle: stranded on Mars? Solve for water with hydrazine and potatoes. Soft sci-fi (e.g., Ursula K. Le Guin’s The Left Hand of Darkness) bends society, not equations—exploring gender fluidity on an alien world where humans have no fixed sex.

Real-world impact? Cell phones (Star Trek’s communicators), tablets (2001: A Space Odyssey), and even the internet (Neuromancer’s cyberspace) were sci-fi before they were real. The genre fails when it ignores rules (e.g., faster-than-light travel without explaining energy costs), but thrives when it stresses details: Kim Stanley Robinson’s Mars Trilogy spends pages on terraforming soil chemistry.

Three related subjects are cyberpunk’s tech rebellions, afrofuturism’s cultural time machines, and how NASA uses sci-fi for training.
